Berks County Wine Trail

Set among the rolling countryside of Southeastern Pennsylvania, the Berks County Wine Trail brings together a diverse collection of twelve wineries, each offering its own unique approach to winemaking. Established in 2005, the trail has grown into a popular destination for visitors from across Pennsylvania and beyond, showcasing the region’s dynamic and evolving wine scene.

Visit www.berkscountywinetrail.com to learn more

title: At These California Vineyards, Truffles (Almost) Steal the Show
summary:
Estate-grown truffles offer new ways for vineyard owners to diversify their production and income streams.

The post At These California Vineyards, Truffles (Almost) Steal the Show appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
“This is a big one.”

It was a cool January morning at Tufera Farms, in the hills east of Paso Robles, and vintner Austin Hope was carefully clawing through the moist soil with his hands and a long trowel. He’d been digging for nearly 10 minutes since his dog, Nyx, had sniffed out a prized black Périgord truffle.

The farm’s bounty only became apparent recently, seven years after 1,500 holly oak trees—their roots inoculated with the fungus to grow truffles—were planted. The property’s 200-plus acres of vineyard were planted more than 20 years ago, and its Cabernet Sauvignon forms the backbone of Hope’s eponymous bottling.

When the land came on the market last year, he wasn’t looking to buy another vineyard. “But the truffles were here,” says Hope, who was impressed by a 1.8-pound black Périgord that was unearthed there in 2024, likely the largest ever in North America, with an estimated value of $3,800. “I was very jealous.”

After his family purchased the property, Hope trained his duck-hunting dog to search for truffles, and they started hunting together this past winter. On this particular morning, he was joined by many of Paso Robles’ top chefs and restaurateurs, who he hopes will become reliable truffle customers one day.

“We’re in peak season right now,” said Stasi Seay, who manages Tufera Farms. “The cool thing is that you get them so fresh here,” she said. Truffles only have a 48-hour window of optimal flavor, leaving little room for error when shipped from Europe, where they grow in the wild.

After more gentle digging, Hope eventually found the truffle’s edges. It was a whopper. “You didn’t know you were taking a bowling ball out!” Seay said, encouraging everyone to get on their knees and take a whiff of the truffle in situ. “Where else do you get 20 people to stick their heads in a hole?” she laughed. “You’re gonna love it. It’s truffy!”

It was a fortuitous day. Hope and his team, plus Aiko Vail and her German Shepherd, Flora, from Truffle Dog Company, dug up four large truffles. They were cleaned and weighed, and their darkness and marbling were documented; that’s how commercial truffles are scored before they hit the market.

Hope shaved off some slices and served them on bread with truffle butter. Not a bad breakfast for a Monday morning.

Finding the truffles is step one, and it’s a challenge. Tufera Farms is one of three vineyards in California reliably harvesting black Périgords every winter, along with Caelesta Vineyard, also in Paso Robles, and Presqu’ile, in Santa Barbara County.

Only now are these vintners figuring out step two, which is also tricky: How to successfully integrate this highly perishable fungus into your winery business.

Hope plans to properly enter that phase next year, using truffles in products for his recently launched Hope Provisions Co. “We want to take it national,” he said of the new brand, which is already producing estate olive oil, wildflower honey, barbecue rubs, and finishing salts.

Caelesta, just a few miles away from Tufera, is further along in their truffle journey. The Farrell family planted 10 acres of inoculated holly oaks in 2015 and found their first truffles about seven years later. The harvests have grown since then, totaling 35 pounds this year.

“Truffles have such a short life,” said Brian Farrell Jr., who manages the property and also makes the wine. “How do you make them last throughout the year?”

January and February are the heart of the brief season, and this winter he hosted hunts followed by truffle-forward brunches and dinners. “They were sold out by Christmas,” said Farrell, who also leads the occasional truffle cooking demo.

He’s enlisted the chefs at Oak & Vine Catering to make truffle butter. “That’s the one thing I can make and freeze,” he said. “With everybody now, we serve truffle butter and bread.” They’re also tinkering with a truffle cheese idea. “It’s trial and error,” he said.

Farrell is fascinated by how truffles can influence other foods via osmosis, like how eggs stored near truffles become infused with their flavor. He recently visited Cartwright Truffière in Oregon, where he tried regular macarons and then macarons that had sat next to truffles. “They didn’t have that truffle smell or taste at all, but they were 100 times better,” he said. “It’s nature’s MSG.”

Down the coast in the Santa Maria Valley, Presqu’ile Vineyard unearthed 6.5 pounds of truffles this year from their less-than-two-acre orchard. That doesn’t sound like a lot, until you realize that there are 454 grams to a pound, and that a single gram of fresh black Périgord truffle can fetch $5.

Once the Presqu’ile orchard is at full force, they’re expecting about 40 pounds of truffles annually. “That means more than we can possibly use,” said Estate Manager Andrew Heilbrun. Truffles are integrated into the winery’s menu by Chef Julie Simon, and Heilbrun plans to sell the rest to nearby restaurants, like the Michelin-starred Bell’s in Los Alamos.

The rush to plant truffle orchards across California is only intensifying. Few have a closer view of the action than Alana McGee, founder of Truffle Dog Company, which sends teams of experts and dogs around the country to help orchard owners find truffles. She’s seen “a bunch” of new orchards being planted in Northern California and believes the current projects are more likely to harvest truffles than the wave of less meticulous plantings that happened 10 to 15 years ago.

“People who come in now, if they’re getting good advice, they are in a better position to ramp up faster,” said McGee. “That doesn’t mean bucketloads at year five, but they might get some earlier.”

No matter what, the orchards require constant attention. “If you don’t manage it actively, it won’t work,” she said. “There’s not a lot you have to do, but you have to do it, and you have to do it in a certain timeframe. It’s a two-year cycle, so if you don’t, you’ve screwed yourself for two years.”

Jessica and Matt Locklin are learning this quickly. They purchased land with an existing orchard close to Tufera Farms and now manage the project while working full-time in the wine business. The orchard was planted seven years ago, but they haven’t found a truffle yet.

They hope to one day sell their truffles directly to high-end restaurants. “Things are promising, but unless you’re constantly checking the ground and having dogs come out, it’s really hard to know if this is a viable operation,” said Jessica, who works for Paso Robles Wine Country Alliance, noting that every step of the process, from hiring dogs to inoculating the trees, is expensive. “We fund this with our own savings.”

They work with the American Truffle Company, whose cofounder Robert Chang employs cutting-edge research to ensure that his clients’ orchards bear fruit. Of the more than 350 properties across the United States that have tried to grow truffles over the past 40 years, Chang said the vast majority fail. Most of his clients are not vineyards, but he’s currently seeing an “explosion” of vineyards seeking new revenue streams during the wine industry’s slump.

“Truffles are a great way to diversify,” said Chang, who also founded the Napa Truffle Festival in 2010. “Vineyards have the infrastructure. They have the labor. They have the equipment. They have the land. They have the water. Truffles are high-end, and that complements their brands very well.”

He’s worked with prominent Northern California properties like Raymond Vineyards, Peju Winery, and Donum Estate, though none of his wine-country clients have harvested truffles yet. But that hasn’t stopped them from getting wine customers ready. Manzanita Grove Vineyard, for instance, is already hosting truffle events, years before their orchard is likely to be harvested. “People are not waiting until the production of truffles to get into truffle tourism,” said Chang.

Back in downtown Paso Robles, Austin Hope’s morning ended with lunch at BL Brasserie, where Chef Laurent Grangien was nearly giddy at the sight of a just-plucked truffle. He disappeared into the kitchen and returned a few minutes later with a dish of warm beets, hazelnuts, and goat cheese. He shaved the truffle into a generous pile of thin slivers on top. The dish was ambrosial, rich, earthy, and somehow fruity—intriguingly delicious flavors for which words do little justice, much like a fine wine. It only took one bite to realize what all this fuss is about.

title: In the War Against Lanternflies, East Coast Wineries Are Getting Creative
summary:
As spotted lanternflies continue to torment vineyards, vintners and community members are coming up with creative solutions and retaliating together.

The post In the War Against Lanternflies, East Coast Wineries Are Getting Creative appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Nothing brings a community together quite like a common enemy—and the wine community has a lot of enemies at present: tariffs, inflation, climate change, anti-alcohol sentiment, changing consumer behavior, and plain-old competition from an increasing number of beverage options. With wineries as with Hollywood, though, the more compelling battle often isn’t the economic, cultural, or even meteorological one, but the one fought against a hostile alien invasion.

Enter: spotted lanternflies. An antagonist with as dramatic a costume as the best cinematic villains, this invasive insect species is believed to have arrived on our shores in 2012, were first identified in Pennsylvania in 2014, and have wreaked havoc in certain Northeastern wine regions and vineyards since the early 2020s.

“Their primary food source is the tree of heaven—also an invasive species,” says Beth Erickson, President and CEO of Virginia’s Loudoun Convention & Visitors Association, where spotted lanternflies have enthusiastically taken up residence. “Their secondary food source is grapevines.”

The First Attack

Through nutrient-sucking, sap-secreting, and other destructive behaviors, spotted lanternfly populations are affecting everything in wineries from vine health and fruit quality to visitor experience. 

The first wave of doing battle with these winged tormentors involved a lot of crossed fingers, in places like the Finger Lakes to the Garden State and beyond. “It blew up pretty quickly,” says Dustin Tarpine, chairman of Garden State Wine Growers Association, and co-founder of Vinetech Vineyard Management Company, “and it took a few years to figure a lot of stuff out.”

Armed with some input from various academic programs and state departments of agriculture, many wineries with initial infestations resorted to various sprays to try to keep the bugs down. “The same materials that we would use to control the few pests that we do have, fortunately do work on lanternflies as well,” says Tarpine, though those methods can prove a quandary for organic wineries, or those whose ethos would typically prohibit them. “We generally don’t use insecticides but may be forced to this year,” says Michael Migliore, co-owner of Hudson Valley’s Whitecliff Vineyard and Winery.

Meanwhile, the introduction of would-be lanternfly predators to vineyards has had mixed results. “If I could figure out a way to make lanternflies more attractive to chickens, I would,” says Scott Spelbring, head winemaker at Bluemont Vineyard, an extension of Virginia’s Great Country Farms, which already has the birds in place. “[The chickens] haven’t really wanted anything to do with them.”

Scraping the lanternfly egg sacks before they have a chance to release nymphs is also a common strategy, though a labor-intensive one for vineyard workers. “Our vineyard stewards are the first line of defense; ensuring they are properly trained to identify and manage spotted lanternflies across all life stages is essential,” says Leo Mora, production winemaker at Long Island’s Sparkling Pointe Vineyards & Winery. “To support this, the team at Cornell Integrated Pest Management and Raul E. Lemus Garza, (Bilingual Project Specialist) have developed Spanish-language pest management resources and training,” he says, which have been attended by the vineyard teams of various wineries in Long Island and beyond.

Increasing Consumer Awareness

As lanternflies prove both a nuisance to the public at-large and a frequent source of headlines, consumers are also becoming more aware of them, which helps the cause, to a degree. “Finger Lakes gets a lot of tourists from areas that are already infested with spotted lanternflies,” says Shannon Brock, co-owner and estate manager of Silver Thread Vineyard, “so whenever the topic comes up, I encourage people to get their cars washed before driving up here so they don’t inadvertently bring eggs masses with them.”

In other good news, many of the wineries in the northeast that first did battle against these bespeckled predators have seen a drop in numbers after the initial infestation. “The issue has abated for us a bit,” says Tarpine. “So far it’s followed a pretty consistent pattern: they come in, you have a three-ish year period where it’s bad, and then they tend to migrate out somewhat.”

“I would say 2023 was probably the peak of how bad it got for us,” Tarpine continues. “We’re still seeing them every year, but it’s something that we’re just kind of living with now; it’s not like the issue that it was, or quite the volume that it was a couple years ago.”

As certain wineries are now in the declining stage of lanternfly infestations, others are gearing up for their own battles, better armed and benefitting from all that has already been learned.

“Many vineyards, including ours, have bug traps around their plantings to monitor for spotted lanternflies,” says Brock. “Most vineyards have been aggressively cutting down tree of heaven on their properties in order to reduce potential populations, and all of our vineyard employees have been trained in spotting egg masses and the different stages of the insect…. As a practicing organic vineyard, we purchased an organic insecticide that was effective last season so that we could gain experience using it in advance of the expected infestation.”

Loudoun County’s ‘Scrape for the Grape’ Initiative

“I was walking my dog, and there was a couple having photos taken right after their wedding,” recalls Erickson. In Loudoun County, Virginia—D.C.’s wine country—wineries are a common setting for such celebrations. “But then the bride drops her groom’s hand, steps away, lifts up the hem of her gown, and stomps on a spotted lanternfly,” she recounts. “And as she did it, she said, ‘This is for the vineyards.’”

The realization that visitors were already built-in allies in the war on lanternflies, and a source of potential community action, was an “aha” moment for Erickson and Loudoun. “We were coming into the 40th anniversary of wine in Loudoun County in 2024, and we knew that we were going to be marking that, and were trying to figure out a way to connect and to really make people aware that we truly did need all hands on deck,” she says, after spotted lanternflies came on pretty rapidly to the area in 2023, progressing southward and westward from the northeast’s initial infestations.

Partnering with Loudoun Invasive Removal Alliance, (LIRA) an organization founded in 2023 which represents 86 homeowners associations, 16 wineries and vineyards, and Ida Lee Park, Loudoun’s Scrape for the Grape initiative was born, gathering volunteers to sift through vineyards and nearby parks before budbreak to destroy egg masses before they hatch.  

“It’s the most disgusting Easter egg hunt,” Erickson says.

Building a Lanternfly-Destroying Army

Like any good egg hunt, however, Scrape for the Grape does bring people out en masse, growing from just over 400 volunteers in its first year in 2024, to more than 1100 volunteers in 2026. Between LIRA, Loudoun Wildlife, and Virginia Tech’s Virginia Cooperative Extension, webinars were developed to educate volunteers both about the spotted lanternfly lifecycle, and the identification of the tree of heaven. Volunteers can sign up to work at a specific winery, or be assigned to one, where they’ll work in small teams to identify, count, and destroy egg masses, using a plastic card to scrape them off of the vines and crush them in the process.

At Bluemont Vineyard, Spelbring hosts 50 volunteers on each of the three Scrape days. “People are usually squeamish about bugs, but to see families, kids, dogs, and everybody come out for this, it’s really been impactful,” he says. “For some people, it’s the first time they’ve actually had their hands on vines, so we get a lot of questions and it’s actually been a lot of fun.” With that hard work comes rewards for participants: Volunteers may be treated to cider and donuts, discounts, or free wine tastings on the Scrape for the Grape days, or ongoing discounts and promotions throughout the season.

The reality of lanternfly lifestyle, however, is that the number of destroyed egg masses in a vineyard doesn’t necessarily correlate to a decrease in lanternfly adults present in that same vineyard later in the season. “My understanding is they do like to switch food sources, so they may start in the vineyard, and then they’re like, ‘all right, we’re bored here,’ and then vice versa,” Spelbring says. 

Regardless, based on volunteer-reported counts of egg sacks destroyed, using a conservative average of about 30 eggs per sack, “over the three years of the program, there have been over 2300 volunteers who have come out in total, and they have eliminated nearly 20 million spotted lanternflies,” says Erickson. “No matter where they are, they certainly don’t recognize any kind of boundaries,” she says, “so the more that can be removed out of the entire ecosystem, the better for all of us.”

Whether or not scraping has made a difference in the number of lanternflies found in vineyards, “the big benefit that’s come out of all of this is the sense of community and overall awareness,” says Spelbring. “It strengthens the ties between the winery and the visitors.”

Erickson agrees, pointing out that “the wineries have all seen an increase in their wine club memberships,” since they have a personal, tangible stake in the success of the winery for whom they’ve done battle. 

Meanwhile, Erickson and the various partner organizations are preparing Scrape for the Grape to be a blueprint for other regions and wineries in the future, knowing that Loudoun’s infestation will eventually taper off, and invasions elsewhere will amp up. 

“I’ve spoken with my colleagues in other regions, and everybody’s watching for it,” she says, even as far away as California. “It’s just a drop in the wine barrel, if you will,” she says, “but it’s awareness, it’s engagement, it’s all the things about volunteering and sustainability that matter.”

title: Is Mexican Nebbiolo… Actually Nebbiolo?
summary:
The vast differences between Nebbiolo in Mexico and Piedmont have led winemakers and industry pros to wonder if they’re even the same grape.

The post Is Mexican Nebbiolo… Actually Nebbiolo? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
One gorgeous spring day in Valle de Guadalupe, as a band played a romantic Mexican folk song, I tasted two Nebbiolos under a 450-year-old oak tree at Casa Magoni winery. Or perhaps I should say I tasted one Nebbiolo and one “Nebbiolo”—in big air quotes.

I was with the 85-year-old winemaker Camillo Magoni, who poured two different bottlings. First, his 2016 Nebbiolo Clone 34, grown from vines native to Valtellina in Italy’s Lombardy region, where Magoni was born. Next came his 2019 Nebbiolo de Baja. The difference was stark: Clone 34 had the light color, finesse, and rose and cherry notes of a young Piemontese Nebbiolo. The Nebbiolo de Baja was darker, more muscular, and more brooding. I enjoyed both, but the question was unavoidable: How were these two wines from the same grape variety?

“Of course they are different,” Magoni said. “But I never have a preference as long as the wine is well made.”

Other Baja winemakers are more blunt about their skepticism toward the so-called Nebbiolo that grows in Baja. “Some people say that it’s Dolcetto or Lambrusco,” said Lulú Martinez Ojeda, winemaker at Bruma, one of Valle de Guadalupe’s most highly regarded producers. “All I can say is it’s not Nebbiolo. I love it, but it’s not Nebbiolo.” Martinez Ojeda says a Mexican-American sommelier in New York once asked about her wine, “So is this Nebbiolo, or is this Nebbio-cholo?”

So, if the Nebbiolo in Valle de Guadalupe is not really Nebbiolo, then what is it?

Like most up-and-coming wine regions, Valle de Guadalupe has struggled to find a signature grape. The region’s drought conditions and heat lead to a lot of experimentation, and many vineyards grow Chardonnay and Cabernet Sauvignon. Some grow very good Chenin Blanc. Some believe Rhône varieties like Mourvèdre and Grenache do best. Others veer toward Spanish varieties like Tempranillo. Others still insist on Italian grapes like Sangiovese, Montepulciano, and Aglianico.

But for now, the signature grape in Valle de Guadalupe is Nebbiolo. L.A. Cetto, the region’s big legacy producer, has promoted Nebbiolo as the area’s main variety since the 1980s, and it’s been in Baja for at least seven decades. After World War II, many varieties were imported here from Piedmont, including Barbera, Grignolino, Freisa, and “one variety they named Nebbiolo,” Magoni said, with a smirk.

Magoni came to Baja in the 1960s after working in vineyards in Valtellina, a valley in Italy’s Lombardy region, where Nebbiolo is the local grape. “Nebbiolo is probably the variety I know better than any other, so when I arrived in Baja and visited the wineries, I was a little bit shocked,” he said. “Of course, I had my ideas. But I shut my mouth and just observed and didn’t talk.”

The Nebbiolo you taste in Baja is quite different from what you’d recognize in Italian wines made from it, like Barolo or Barbaresco. It’s darker, less tannic, and often has a briny, saline character. Maybe it’s the climate, or the result of growing Nebbiolo in a drought, where the water that remains on the valley’s ocean-bed soil becomes saltier and poorer in quality.

“It’s not Italian. There’s more color, more intensity,” said Paolo Paoloni, an Italian winemaker working for Villa Montefiori in Baja. Paoloni calls his Nebbiolo de Guadalupe. Meanwhile, Magoni has adopted the name Nebbiolo de Baja, presumably in case some DNA testing reveals the grape’s true identity.

Kristin Magnussen at Vinos Lechuza recently released a Nebbiolo aged in stainless steel and sold in a can. She offered a hypothesis: “In Piedmont, it’s a light- skinned grape used to fog. But here, they’re in direct sunlight. Maybe they’ve evolved to have a thicker skin,” she said. “Is it really Nebbiolo? Well, it’s Mexican Nebbiolo.”

“Nobody expects anything mainstream from Mexico, so we can make whatever we want,” said Martinez Ojeda. We were sipping her vibrant Casa Jipi Nebbiolo, which is bursting with fresh red fruit and a touch of minerality. The Nebbiolo for Casa Jipi comes from a vineyard planted in 1978. “Casa Jipi is definitely a Nebbio-cholo. At this point, it’s just a part of us.”

There is a long tradition of grapes being called by confusing local names, especially with Italian varieties. Sangiovese is variously called Brunello, Morellino, Prugnolo Gentile, and two dozen other names. Nebbiolo is called Chiavennasca in Valtellina and Spanna in Gattinara.

Cases of grape identity crisis happen more often than you’d think. The most famous was in Chile, where they discovered that the strange Merlot they were growing was actually Carménère. A similar discovery happened in Colli Berici, in Italy’s Veneto. There, red wines once thought to be Cabernet Franc also turned out to be Carménère, based on DNA testing. While some wineries embraced Carménère as a new signature grape, not all local winemakers were happy about this revelation. Many were Carménère deniers who continued to bottle their wines as Cabernet del Veneto.

Something similar may soon happen in Valle de Guadalupe. I spoke with José Vouillamoz, one of the world’s foremost grape geneticists and co-author of the groundbreaking encyclopedia Wine Grapes. He said at least one DNA test on Baja Nebbiolo showed it could be the grape Lambrusco di Alessandria, which has origins in Piedmont’s Alessandria province. However, Vouillamoz made clear that this was only one sample from one vineyard, and that more testing would be needed before anything definitive could be established. “I can’t say anything until I see the results of DNA profiling,” he said. “Maybe let them know I do perform DNA testing on a regular basis.”

“So why hasn’t there been a DNA test?” said Martinez Ojeda. “Well, that’s very expensive. But maybe it’s time.” She added, “If you find out that it’s not Nebbiolo—that it’s Dolcetto or Freisa or whatever—then what? Then what do you do?”

Back at Casa Magoni, as our tasting under the old oak tree moved into a leisurely afternoon of wine drinking, I proposed a similar question: “So, have you done DNA testing?

Magoni laughed. “Yeah,” he said.

“And?”

“Who cares?” he said with a shrug.

title: Malaysia’s Indigenous Rice Wine Is Poised for Cocktail Greatness
summary:
Next-gen tuak producers are showing that traditional drinks can and should be part of Malaysia’s contemporary culinary landscape.

The post Malaysia’s Indigenous Rice Wine Is Poised for Cocktail Greatness appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
One Gawai, an indigenous harvest festival celebrated in East Malaysia, Raffly Langi returned to his family’s hometown in Sarawak, on the island of Borneo. He met up with a friend-of-a-friend from West Malaysia, Ayden Chong, for several glasses of the mildly-alcoholic brewed rice drink, tuak. “Japan has sake, Korea has soju, China has baijiu… what do we as Malaysians have?” Chong pondered.

Looking down at their glasses, they realised they had the answer in their hands. That was the beginning of both a friendship and a new drinks brand: Tuak Alus (”alus” means “fine,” riffing on “fine wine”). 

The pair’s next-generation tuak is rooted in both tradition and a sense of experimentation. “We conducted numerous R&D sessions experimenting with different ragi [yeast], rice, and sugar,” says Chong. The brand has released special editions infused with pandan leaves, as well as a sour plum version. “For us, it’s never just about selling alcohol; we’re sharing a piece of Dayak cultural heritage,” he says. (“Dayak” is a collective term for a group of indigenous peoples of Sarawak.) “We want people to understand the origins, traditions, and stories behind it.” 

The glutinous rice wine is tied to rituals at Gawai, where it is offered to gods, as well as many other festivities and occasions, including drinking games that happen at family parties. Tuak is now moving beyond the longhouses and villages and finding a home in restaurants and cocktail bars across Malaysia, in part thanks to younger producers like Chong and Langi.

Ripe for Experimentation

There are many different ways of making tuak across Dayak communities. Recipes vary between villages and individuals, creating variations of sweetness, texture, and alcohol content. But the fundamentals are the same: the brewers, who have learned the art from their elders, soak rice in water, traditionally in a running stream, for up to ten hours. The mixture is cooked in bamboo over an open fire at low heat, then spread evenly out to dry and mixed with wild yeasts to start the fermentation. Everything goes into airtight earthenware jars for two weeks before the liquid is bottled. Many brewers then add cold water and continue fermentation for another two weeks; the Iban (a Dayak group) sometimes place glass bottles above the fireplace to brew a stronger batch.

Tuak Alus started small and grew as Chong and Langi’s friends and family began placing orders. Langi, who is of Iban heritage, acts as the master brewer and Chong leads operations. They source ingredients from Sarawak, such as gula apong (local palm sugar), which creates a smoothtexture and aroma—“not just sweetness, but a fragrance that blends beautifully with the yeast and glutinous rice,” says Chong.

Another new brand called Ngirupp, named after the Iban word “to drink” (ngirup), was founded by Chinese-Sarawakian bartender Sylvia Lim in 2023. After trying a tuak made by her sister-in-law’s Iban mother, Lim became deeply fascinated by the brewing process. That particular bottle, aged for five years, had a smooth, dry profile and changed Lim’s perspective of what tuak could be.

Lim, who also runs many Sarawak-based events promoting tuak cocktails, uses traditional methods and sources local ingredients, with ragi made in-house. “My tuak is a balance between tradition and modernization,” she says. “In terms of appearance and flavor, it’s not something you would typically drink. However, we preserve the traditional brewing methods and belief systems passed down through generations—not moving the fermentation jars before the tuak is ready, and avoiding sour foods while brewing…”

As tuak’s popularity grows,  it’s naturally finding its way into cocktails—just as sake, soju, and baiju did. To award-winning Malaysian bartender Shawn Chong, his country’s abundance of distinctive local ingredients, like torch ginger, nutmeg, and kedondong (ambarella/local plum), allows for a high level of creativity. Although Malaysian alcoholic beverages are harder to access for local bartenders, due to the difficulty small brewers have of obtaining licenses, these new tuak makers are showing that traditional drinks can and should be a part of the contemporary culinary Malaysian landscape. “There was a lot of introspection happening during Covid,” Shawn says. “In the case of tuak, which traditionally there is a custodian of the ‘family recipe,’ the younger generation were asking the question: What happens if those recipes get lost?” 

Beyond Borders

Tuak’s influence goes beyond Sarawak. In Kuala Lumpur, the two Michelin-starred Dewakan serves Endu Roselle, a sparkling Iban-style tuak made from highly prized Bario rice, with roselle petals. Raz Ng, co-founder of Kuala Lumpur’s Bar None, sees tuak as a representation of fermentation techniques that have always existed in Malaysian cultures. “The goal is for our drinks to feel lived-in, Southeast Asian, and still very much contemporary” she says. Using components like tuak is part of building a cocktail program that focuses on familiarity—nostalgia, texture, and memory—as well as innovation. 

Yes, there are a growing number of tuak producers across Sarawak and West Malaysia, but it remains difficult for producers to get licenses to sell alcohol. As a result, most are small-batch brewers, depending on authorities that turn a blind eye. For the category to mature and independent brands to grow, there needs to be supportive legislation. Not only will it allow for the current group of producers to thrive, but it will give opportunity to those brewing outside of the main urban hubs, including more Dayak brewers across the different groups, to be part of the movement.

title: The Best Wine Pairings for Summer BBQs, Picnics, and More
summary:
Across patio grilling sessions, beach picnics, and campfire cookouts, these sommeliers agree that the best summer pairings are the least fussy ones.

The post The Best Wine Pairings for Summer BBQs, Picnics, and More appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Summer wine pairings are built differently than winter ones. Few people sitting outside in 89-degree temperatures want to chat about tertiary aromas over a perfectly composed entrée. Instead, summer drinking is pleasantly lawless. Plates are mismatched or made of paper, while chips gather moisture from pool-dipped hands and quickly go stale in the heat. Someone is always losing the corkscrew, and everyone is impatiently waiting for the bottle of rosé to hit the ideal temperature. 

All of this charming chaos is also what makes summer wine pairing so fun. Instead of precision, the best warm-weather wines tend to prioritize versatility, refreshment, and ease. 

“Summer wine should feel like a playlist, not homework,” says André Hueston Mack, sommelier and founder of Maison Noir Wines. And across patio grilling sessions, seafood boils, beach picnics, and campfire cookouts, these sommeliers and chefs tend to agree that the best summer pairings are the least fussy ones.

Backyard Barbecues

The Foods: Burgers, hot dogs, grilled chicken, ribs, steak, kabobs, potato salad, chips

The Wines: Chillable reds, rosé, Syrah, and GSM blends

Backyard barbecue may be America’s most forgiving wine occasion—and also its messiest. Juicy burgers soak through napkins and paper plates. Sticky ribs compete for table space with bowls of potato salad and cobs of corn. Someone burns a hot dog and someone else conveniently prefers it that way. 

“For burgers and hot dogs, I love a chillable red like Gamay or a juicy Pinot Noir because they’ve got enough acidity to cut through the fat without feeling heavy in the heat,” says Mack. “If steaks are hitting the grill, Syrah becomes the move, especially something peppery and smoky that mirrors the char.”

Rosé also earns its perennial summer status at the grill because it can easily pivot between grilled chicken, spice rubs, and sweeter barbecue sauces. Mack calls it the ultimate flexible option, “whether there’s barbecue sauce, dry rub, or something spicy involved.”

Sommelier and wine educator Jade Palmer leans on Rhône-style GSM blends when it comes to barbecue’s bigger flavors. “The combination of ripe red fruit, light florals, and lots of spice notes in a GSM lends itself well to be paired with BBQ,” she says.

Seafood Nights

The Foods: Clam bakes, lobster rolls, grilled shrimp, seafood boils, oysters, melted butter

The Wines: Muscadet, Albariño, Assyrtiko, sparkling wine, mineral-driven whites

These are summer occasions with a capital ‘S.’ Think newspaper-covered tables piled high with crab and lobster claws, grilled shrimp, lemon wedges, and corn on the cob. It’s the only time having your own personal bowl of butter is officially sanctioned. 

“This is where high-acid white wines absolutely shine,” says Mack. “Muscadet, Assyrtiko, Albariño—wines that feel salty, citrusy, and refreshing almost taste like they were born near the ocean.” Seafood boils and clam bakes need wines with enough acidity to refresh the palate without disappearing beneath garlic, spice, or smoke.

“If you are a ‘dip everything in melted butter’ sort, a balanced, New World Chardonnay makes a great counterpoint with great acidity to cut through that melty goodness,” says Leonetta wine director Torrey Grant, while Palmer opts for textured coastal whites like Muscadet with lees aging. “The bright, citrusy acidity makes it great to pair with shellfish,” she says.

And while seafood pairings often default to still white wines, several experts argue that sparkling wine may be the most versatile of all. “Nothing cuts through drawn butter and shellfish better than bubbles,” says Mack.

Sunset Hikes & Snacky Outdoor Hangs

The Foods: Trail mix, potato chips, olives, tinned fish, cheese and charcuterie, anchovy toast

The Wines: Dry rosé, orange wines and skin-contact whites, Vinho Verde

Sunset hikes and unscheduled outdoor hangs tend to end up with the best kind of dinners—handfuls of slightly crushed chips, good olives, maybe some tinned fish or haphazardly thrown together charcuterie boards. Most aren’t expecting a well-executed pairing as much as they are just grateful that someone thought to bring adult beverages to the occasion.

In the Shop

Barrel-Inspired Acacia Wood and Chalkboard Swivel Cheeseboard Set

In Stock | $89.99

Shop Now

“You want wines that don’t require too much thought and still taste good slightly warmer than intended,” says Mack. “Cheese, chips, olives, trail mix, charcuterie—these are all ‘snack board wines,’ meaning the wine has to flex across a bunch of flavors.”

Brendan Hill, head chef at Tintino in Canberra suggests focusing on portability and lower abv, and getting creative with flavors. “Maybe a little Cantabrian anchovy toast that pairs beautifully with bone-dry Sherry or a light, mineral-driven Greek white like Assyrtiko,” he says. 

For Palmer, skin-contact wines feel suited to golden-hour snacking. “An orange wine with a little tannic grip and tons of citrus and white floral notes is equally refreshing for the hike, and easily paired with snacks like trail mix and popcorn,” she says.

And while proper stemware may be optional, practicality matters. “There are great wines being made in more outdoor-friendly packaging like cans and boxes,” she adds. “No one wants to be weighed down by heavy glass bottles.” Grant also underscores the importance of ease. “Don’t be afraid to search for bottles under screwcap,” says Grant. “You don’t want to keep looking for a wine key if you’re at a picnic or rooting around in your backpack on a hike.”

Beach Picnics

The Foods: Italian subs, fried chicken, fruit platters, seafood snacks, pasta salad, chips

The Wines: Provençal rosé, Vinho Verde, Txakolina, sparkling wines

Beach wines have a pretty straightforward job to do: stay cold, stay refreshing, and don’t knock anyone out after two hours in the sun. This usually means they should have bright acidity, moderate alcohol, and pair well with everything from salty chips and paper-wrapped subs to chilled shrimp and watermelon slices. 

“High acid and lower alcohol matter because heat amplifies alcohol quickly,” says Mack. “Beach wine should pair with the environment as much as the food. Salty air and salty snacks make bright wines taste even better.” This is where those crisp whites, Provençal rosé, Basque Txakolina, and sparkling wines shine—especially styles that feel bright and easygoing rather than overly complex.

In Stock | $25

Shop Now

Grant recommends leaning into wines that match the relaxed energy of the beach itself. “Here the wines should be light, bright wines that work for everyone,” he says. “This is a day of relaxation, not one where you are concentrating too hard on the wines’ pedigree or a perfect pairing. Bring some fresh fruit, a chilled red like a Frappato, and an Arneis … and just have fun!”

Campfires

The Foods: S’mores, grilled sausages, campfire chili, toasted marshmallow, Rice Krispie treats

The Wines: Syrah, Grenache, Zinfandel, Lambrusco, Cabernet Franc

Campfires are one of the few summer occasions where fuller-bodied reds suddenly make sense. The pairings naturally lean smoky and sometimes even a little nostalgic. “Campfire wine is about comfort,” says Mack. 

Syrah, Grenache, and other savory reds tend to shine beside fire-cooked foods and charred sweetness. “For me, there is nothing better than an Hermitage or Côte-Rôtie,” says Grant. “I want dark, brooding wine to go with all the smoke and heat.”

Palmer suggests Cabernet Franc for campfire sipping thanks to its classic balance of juicy fruit and earthy notes, while Hill suggests leaning into slightly chilled Lambrusco with smoky foods. And for the ultimate campfire snack—s’mores? “Maybe a cheeky sip on a nice little glass of Port,” he says.

Outdoor Movie Nights

The Foods: Buttered popcorn, pizza, candy, snack mix, nachos

The Wines: Sparkling rosé, Prosecco, Riesling

Outdoor movie nights are less about serious pairing strategy and more about easy access and generosity. With pizza boxes piling up and bites alternating between gummy worms and popcorn, the goal is to have a good time while still managing to catch the plot of the summer’s latest streaming blockbuster. 

“Popcorn is secretly an amazing wine pairing food because salt and butter make almost everything taste better,” says Mack. “Honestly, this is one of those moments where wine should support the memory, not dominate the conversation.” 

Sparkling wines and wines with a touch of residual sugar are also useful in this setting because they can move easily between salty and sweet snacks. “Prosecco or a slightly off-dry German Riesling work well,” says Hill. 

Grant takes the idea one, slightly poetic step further, suggesting pairing wines directly to the films themselves: A Tuscan wine for Under the Tuscan Sun, Condrieu with Ratatouille, or a quirky Cabernet Franc alongside Young Frankenstein.

Ultimately, the best summer wine pairings are mostly about paying attention to the people gathered around the table, beach blanket, campfire, or cooler. “Summer is probably the best reminder that wine is supposed to support connection,” says Mack. “The best bottle outdoors is usually the one people actually finish.”

title: Go Ahead—Judge Wine by Its Label
summary:
Wine labels are becoming more Instagrammable, expressive, and fun as producers catch up to what young drinkers want.

The post Go Ahead—Judge Wine by Its Label appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
After a string of TikToks touted La Vieille Ferme Rosé in 2024, Famille Perrin, owners of Château de Beaucastel, were faced with a conundrum. Consumers—particularly newer, younger ones—didn’t know how to pronounce the name of their wine. Instead, internet minions were calling the decades-old brand—which has earned widespread recognition for being an approachable, inexpensive and consistently well-made rosé—“chicken wine,” so-named because of the chicken illustration on the label.

But rather than letting it bristle their feathers, the makers leaned in. A few months later, they launched a new limited-edition label. The words “La Vieille Ferme Rosé” are nowhere to be found; in their place, “The Chicken Wine” is prominently emblazoned. The rebrand has so far delighted TikTok users across the U.S. and U.K., who have embraced the new label.

“Wine can still be such a scary, antiquated ‘white tablecloth’ category for so many people,” says Sande Friedman, a wine writer based in Philadelphia. “Younger consumers are far more vocal about that than previous generations … and admit openly that we shop with our eyes and are more open to change than previous generations may have been. A generation of people shopping for ‘the chicken wine’ really showed that power.”

The Power of Visuals

Indeed, the Chicken Wine phenomenon proves, once again, that visuals matter, whether we want to admit it or not. In turn, wine manufacturers are placing new weight on what goes on a label, from the illustrations and typeface to wine descriptors and buzzwords.

“Instructional buzzwords can actually help inform customers of how the winemaker would enjoy this product themselves,” says Friedman. “‘Chillable’ is a word you see a lot … that helps inform people that red wine can be chilled.”

And colorful labels with playful fonts aren’t just frivolous details. Much like a shop’s window dressing can lure in foot traffic, these eye-catching labels can be the difference between making a sale and, well, not. Consumer research backs this up, suggesting that both colors and fonts play significant roles in reeling in new customers. “There’s been an undeniable shift in the look of wine labels in recent years,” Friedman says. “It’s in tandem with what you see happening in soda, snacks, all the ‘fun’ food categories. [There are] bright colors, buzzwords, flash and pomp.”

A Generational Shift

The shift in buying habits is notedly generational. For many millennial and Gen Z consumers, demand for wine has shifted, softened, and evolved in a post-pandemic timeline, says Kathryn Klos, a North Carolina-based sommelier turned travel advisor specializing in wine tourism. 

“For younger generations, who are often less wealthy, experienced and perhaps less ‘serious’ about wine, a playful or artful label signals approachability,” she says. “They know their friends or followers might not know the difference between Montepulciano and Brunello di Montalcino, but they sure as hell know a cute label.”

Klos says this isn’t to minimize the wine knowledge of younger generations. “But the truth is, fewer of us can afford to drink wine as a serious hobby the way older generations could,” she stresses. From a stocking perspective, listening to this customer feedback is critical for winemakers looking to stay relevant.

“It’s a very important driver in the current market,” says Friedman, explaining that some importers are supporting the design of tiny producers’ fun labels to help little cuvées from native grapes stand out.

It’s also convincing more makers to incorporate UPC barcodes into their labels, which can help streamline inventory management and more accurately track products throughout the supply chain. “Big, huge distributors are noticing and copying, which is why you see this everywhere now,” she says.

New Styles, New Label Best Practices

Natural wines—a broad category without a firm definition—are leading the modern label charge. One possible explanation: Many producers, especially those in Europe, are legally prohibited from naming the wine’s regional designation or the specific vineyard in which it was made. (These wines are often deemed “atypical” for their region and are excluded from these designations.) 

Without being able to capitalize on such selling points, many winemakers instead lean on untraditional, eye-catching branding. Case in point, the delicate, floral- inspired labels from Jurtschitsch, a natural wine producer in Austria’s Kamptal region, and the whimsical, cartoonish labels from Domaine de l’Octavin, a Demeter biodynamic-certified outfit in the Arbois region of the Jura.

There’s plenty of great natural wine out there, made by vintners with high-quality standards. But as hype for natural wine continues to trend, shelves are becoming saturated with natural wines of varying quality, many of them bearing flashy labels slapped with phrases like “low sulfur,” “naked,” and “sugar-free.” 

Critics say these health-adjacent buzzwords are unhelpful at best and misleading at worst. There are scant legal regulations around their use, which, coupled with sexy label art, can confuse consumers, particularly those unfamiliar with classic winemaking. As a result, more faulty wines with off-putting flavors or aromas are entering the marketplace.

“There was a moment in natural wine when labels alone could get you on a shelf and sell bottles,” says Klos. “And unfortunately, the wine inside was bad more often than it should have been.”

The Good vs. Bad of New Labels

So, does the good outweigh the bad in the great label debate? Friedman says cool branding can help mask cruddy wine, but it can also push boundaries in an industry that’s often stuck in its ways.

“Anything that makes people more comfortable shopping is a win for an industry that’s been in a downward trend in recent years,” she says. “It also actually helps people remember wines they’ve enjoyed better, because there’s an easy visual instead of trying to remember a grape or place.”

Plus, Klos says that a fun label means a consumer is more likely to deem a bottle “gram worthy” and perhaps give it some free marketing online. “More often than not, a nice bottle is a splurge—and one that deserves to be documented on social media,” she says. “The game has changed for the next generation of wine drinkers, and art has a lot of power in that game.”

What Makes a Good Label?

Wine consultant Kathryn Klos shares what makes for an effective label these days.

Buzzwords Beyond Buzzwords’ Sake

“When there’s full transparency from a winemaker or brand about farming practices, regenerative choices and winemaking intention, that’s a beautiful thing. But when big brands use unregulated phrases like ‘no chemicals added’ without doing anything different just to make more money, we run into problems.”

Aesthetically Pleasing, but Authentic

“Yes, the label should be art-forward, but art without purpose feels empty and people can sniff that out. There’s nothing wrong with beauty, but when art becomes detached from intention, it risks losing its soul. A wine label can be wild and whimsical, but if it doesn’t echo the spirit of what’s inside, it becomes costume instead of character.”

Storytelling with Honesty

“Just like a great wine reflects its maker and its place, good label copy is an opportunity to extend the story and make the experience more memorable. Ultimately, the intended audience and their values should dictate what that means.”

This article originally appeared in the June/July 2025 California issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. Click here to subscribe today!

title: ‘Don’t Drink the Faerie Wine’ and Other Important Romantasy Lessons
summary:
On the Wine Enthusiast Podcast, we chatted with Spirits Podcast co-hosts Amanda McLoughlin and Julia Schifini about all things fairy wine.

The post ‘Don’t Drink the Faerie Wine’ and Other Important Romantasy Lessons appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
“Don’t drink the Faerie wine.”

This is the advice that human protagonist Feyre Archeron receives upon entering the Fae realm in the best-selling romantasy series A Court of Thorns and Roses. Indeed, the stuff is far stronger than human wine, and when Archeron disregards this excellent advice, she experiences such all-consuming ecstasy she physically cannot stop dancing until the next day.

This March, author Sarah J. Maas announced the upcoming release of the next two ACOTAR books, sending #Booktok into its own all-consuming ecstasy. 

But Faerie lore, and the narrative origins of Faerie wine, go way back—and certainly precede SJM. As long as stories have been told, they’ve included forbidden treasures from other realms that serve as temptations—think the Book of Genesis’ forbidden fruit or Persephone’s cursed pomegranate seeds. In the age of ACOTAR, wine continues to be a trope in romantasy and beyond. (Many fans have latched onto “Don’t drink the Faerie wine,” even slapping it on merchandise like mugs and shirts.)

“A glass of wine is not just a glass of wine… it’s often one of our first introductions in fiction into the idea that things are not quite what they seem,” says the Spirits Podcast co-host and producer Amanda McLoughlin. 

In an effort to dig deeper into the motifs that dominate Faerie wine lore, WE’s intrepid managing print editor Jordon Kaplan sat down with McLoughlin and Spirits Podcast co-host Julia Schifini, who previously joined the Wine Enthusiast Podcast to chat about “witchy” winemaking traditions.

Consider this your required listening before the release of ACOTAR Books Six and Seven.

On the motif of wine in Fae stories

Amanda: “That is basically like a trope of, when you’re in another world, whether that is the Fae realm, the spirit world, or any other dimension of any kind besides the mortal world, you should not eat or drink any food while you’re there because it will lead to something wrong.”

Julia: “Sometimes that means you’re trapped there forever, sometimes it means you’ve been put under the control of the person who fed you, sometimes it means you get turned into another creature or another form, and sometimes it just tastes bad. Or, it tastes so good that it ruins normal food for you forever.”

Amanda: “One of the favorite parts for me about these Fae myths is that you can’t really trust your own senses. And so you might smell or see something delicious when actually it’s rotten underneath, like Julia mentioned—something we see in Holly Black books, for example. Or maybe, as you mentioned, you lose inhibition and not just the, ‘I had a couple glasses of wine and I feel more likely to dance to Shania Twain than I otherwise would,’ but like, ‘No, I’m going to dance all night until my feet are bloody.’ … the idea that a glass of wine is not just a glass of wine, that a platter of fruit is not just a platter of fruit, is often one of our first introductions in fiction into the idea that things are not quite what they seem.”

On the literary metaphor of taking a sip

Julia: “It harkens back to these, like, godlike substances that are meant solely for the gods. And if a human were to drink them or eat them, it usually does not end well for them.”

Amanda: “If we think about Joseph Campbell’s hero’s journey, something we refer to very often over on Spirits, it is saying to somebody, ‘I don’t think you’re ready for this.’ And the protagonist gets to say, ‘Yes, I am’ and whether they correctly assess their, you know, willingness to get into the adventure, to take a sip of the beverage, to take the shot they probably shouldn’t take, but they’re feeling it in the night and they do it anyway. It’s a really wonderful kind of metaphor and maybe an aperitif for a bigger adventure of saying, ‘Well, these problems aren’t mine, and I wasn’t made for this world. I wasn’t made for this journey. But, you know what?’”

On wine as a plot device

Amanda: “I think one great example that we already touched on a bit is the sort of too-good-to-be-true mirage nature of some of these items. And often that is our introduction to a new setting or new world and really the idea of the uncanny, which is something that shows up in a lot of fantasy, sci-fi things where we’re inventing new worlds. That’s something I really love about the romantic trope is it is set in a world similar to ours, but a little bit elevated. And so we will often see this as the sort of first sign for protagonists that something is not quite right.

And also the start or the example of different kinds of morality and justice, because it’s not really right as we live in our mortal world that we would enter into a deal, for example, without… having a very clear idea of the terms. If I do a favor for you and then later I say, well, because I did that favor for you, you have to do something for me, you’d be like, ‘That’s not cool.’ It’s a little bit extortionate, a little bit rude. ‘That’s not how we do things around here. We try to have a fair exchange of value.’ But in the fairy world, whether it’s because you are a mortal entering a space where no one’s explained the rules to you, or if, more commonly, the Faerie folk and the Fae are cunning, are wily, are trying to get something over on you, are tricksters, you are kind of failing a test by not assuming an underhanded motive to the people that show you hospitality and kindness on their faces.”

On the concept of ‘sacred hospitality’

Julia: “This is why the sacred hospitality around Fae lore is so interesting… because in general, mythology revolves around these stories that reinforce ‘sacred hospitality,’ which is this idea that if someone has eaten food under your roof, they must be under your protection. And to break that hospitality is an extreme taboo. Like some of the worst punishments that we see in, let’s say, Greek mythology are when the sacred hospitality has been broken. So what is interesting about Fae lore and ‘don’t eat the Fae food’ and everything like that is that the trope is reversed in that way when we’re talking about the Fae, because that is specifically designed to highlight and emphasize that the Fae are not human. And that is what makes them dangerous, because something that we consider fundamentally a taboo in the human world—that would be like punished by the divine if it were broken—the Fae break that taboo as a rule. It is inherently reversed. And that is what is so scary about the Fae.”

On wine in ACOTAR and modern romantasy

Amanda: “One of the reasons why the fairy food and wine is so popular among modern romantasy is because it’s sexy to share a meal, sexy to share a drink, right? These are tropes that we know. But there is this undercurrent that heightens it all of risk, of danger, of care, and of someone choosing to violate the norms of the world they’re from in order to protect the, you know, the sexy lady who came under their mountains.”

The Guests

Julia Schifini (she/her) is a podcaster, sound designer, voice actor, and editor. She is the host and co-producer of Spirits and editor for This Guy Sucked and American Medieval. She is a founding member of the podcast collective, Multitude.

Amanda McLoughlin (she/they) is the CEO of Multitude, a podcast collective, production company, and ad sales provider. A digital creator since 2004 whose career started in finance and business services, she created Multitude in 2018 to support fellow creators in making a living on their own terms. She was named a Forbes 30 Under 30 Media honoree in 2021, co-hosts the podcast Spirits: Mythology & Folklore, and Executive Produces several shows. She also teaches podcasting at Stony Brook University.

Transcript:

I asked Spirits podcast co-hosts Julia and Amanda, the boozy supernatural experts, back over to the pod to discuss the where, the why, and the what realm of fairy food and wine, a tempting treat to lure unsuspecting humans or a simple offering. Amanda, Julia, I am so excited to have you guys on today to talk about a topic that I am very, very interested in. Welcome.

It’s where everything is topsy turvy, and the things that you think, no matter where you go in the world, somebody will offer you, you know, a clean place to sleep, some bread, some water, you know, something to fill your stomach, help you get to wherever you’re going. Even that is something you can’t count on when you are with creatures that are so different from us, that even this I would argue definition of humanity, which is basic hospitality to others, is broken, and that, of course, that risk can be sexy, right? Even in ACOTAR, right, like you have a guide, there is a character that is helping our mortal to figure out, understand, navigate, stay safe in this world. There are people giving warnings to this younger sister, who is doing her best. She shows her pure heart, and they try to help her in turn. And that is also, I think, one of the reasons why the fairy food and wine is so popular among modern romanticity, because, of course, it’s sexy to share a meal, sexy to share a drink, right? These are tropes that we know, but there is this undercurrent that heightens it all of risk of danger of care, and of someone choosing to violate the norms of the world they’re from in order to protect the, you know, the sexy lady who came under their mountain.

JK

Actually, it’s funny. I was just thinking of another part during ACOTAR between the Fae food and that those hospitality rules are restored. I think a major part of the Fae lore in ACOTAR is they have what’s called the mating bond.

AM

Yes, they do

JK

Your true destined love, and to accept the mating bond, you make a meal for your mate.

AM

You do indeed.

JK

Yeah, so between Faye, hospitable. Outsiders, inhospitable.

JS

Yes, and like that is something that they really drive home, specifically in the first book, that is like the Fae are predatory towards mortals, and like to run into a Fae is established, like in at least the first half of that book as a death sentence, right? You run into a Fae, you are going to be dead, or at the very least they are going to play with you until you die, you know what I mean, and so that is a very, like, they’re separating the like the rules here are for the Fae and Fae alone, and when a human tries to interact with those Fae rules or those Fae laws, that is when things go really, really bad,

AM

Yeah, and like Julia said, that is the point of mythology and folklore. It’s to teach us how to be human, to teach us how to act in society, to help us draw those boundaries between desire, fantasy, impulse, urge, and what we all need to do to live together in a, in a sort of acceptable way. One of my favorite figures from Irish mythology is actually a cousin to the Leprechaun. It’s called the Cluricon, which was…

JS

I was hoping you would talk about this.

AM

It’s specifically a leprechaun style little guy, you know, six inches high, looks like a little gnome who lives in the bottom of breweries and distilleries, and this is a great example, too, of like, okay, a little bit of drink is okay, it’s part of the Irish character. What are you going to do? But excess is excessive, that’s too much, that’s not what we’re going to do. And so, as in many examples of folklore, you know, the Cluricon   sort of exemplifies human tradition and habit gone a little bit too far, and so he’s the example, he’s the one you can point to to say that’s that’s actually a little bit too much, when you like your drink too much, when you like excess too much. I love that he like wears silver buckled shoes, he wears blue stockings. Okay, blue is very expensive fabric in Ireland in the 1700s He, he likes finer things too much, and of course he likes to drink too much, and so for the Cluricon specifically, if you have, as in many Fay lore, a modest and measured relationship with him, he’ll prevent your wine and beer from spilling, he’ll make sure that thieves don’t come and steal. Your wares, he’ll keep the seller really nice. He’ll make sure it’s clean and orderly, and also give you some warning if there is, like, a danger coming toward your household. But if you break that symbiosis, if you, you know, make bad drinks, if you disrespect him, if you disturb him, or if he just turns malicious, then there could be things being broken, animals being frightened, attacking people that offend him, and almost worst of all, spoiling your wine.

JS

I do think it’s really interesting, there is a certain, like, line in which there are fairy creatures that are cool associating with humans, like we talk a lot about that with, like, house brownies or house spirits that are like these spirits that almost have a symbiotic relationship with humans in mythology and society. 

AM

We live in a society, Julia.

JS

But like this idea of like, yes, okay, so like I’ll do these things and you’ll help me out in this way, but the minute that that relationship is soiled, or the minute that the rules are broken, that’s when the chaos breaks loose, right. And then there is this like interpretation of the high fay, right, this idea of like the fairies that are just so out of the realm of like what humanity is, even though they often resemble humanity in some way, and that’s where, like, the confusion arises, especially in the modern romantasy tropes.

JK

I was thinking, you know, a lot of these, even in, you know, spirit depiction, we have, you know, absinthe and the green fairy, and that is bargain that you go into with that green fairy during the consumption of absinthe, that should only be this many, or you know, bad things will happen, you’ll start to hallucinate, and I’m starting to think just like different ways that shows up in drink culture…

JS

For sure, I don’t have a ton of like bad drink lore, unfortunately. Like, obviously we are talking about the sort of like don’t eat the fairy food, don’t drink the fairy wine situation. The only one that I’m like striking on right now is there is a mead of poetry in Norse mythology, where it’s like, if you drink this, if you drink this mead, which has a really messed up origin, it is like a, it’s a man who was made out of the spit of the gods after they signed the truce between the Aesir and the veneer, and so he was like a very knowledgeable, like, sort of like seer type man, and then one day he is visiting these two dwarves, and because he has so much knowledge, they kill him, and they drain out all of his blood, and then they mix it with honey, and they create this mead that anyone who drinks it turns into either a poet or a scholar, and so, like, my thought is, like, while that has terrible, terrible origins that feel very Fae coded, it has a positive outcome of, like, yeah, you just have to sort of give up your humanity by drinking the blood and honey of this man, and therefore you are now a poet or a scholar in the realm of, like, where the gods are at in terms of this artistic ability.

AM

Yeah and you lose your humanity in order to kind of touch that or channel that spark of the divine, which itself is too much for a human mind to hold. After mentioning absence, which is a great example, I came across like this, this painting from 1895 by Albert Mengen, called The Green Muse, where a fairy is like strangling and gouging the eyes out of a poet representing absinthe, on which he has become dependent in order to make his, his art.

JK

Well, I mean, that’s, that’s so, that’s, I mean, this could be an even broader conversation, but you talk about, you know, so many people will say, you know, this particular artist is sober now, and now they don’t make anything that’s good, this like, this notion that we need extra help from outside of ourselves to, in order to create good work and good art is, is really interesting, and that we could pin that on some sort of otherworldly entity, like a green fairy.

AM

Yeah, I think it’s a really misguided, but very understandable human urge for perfection, where I think the point of, like, the fairy food that looks beautiful, but is rotten underneath, or the beverage that is so delicious and tastes of sunshine and moonlight, and everything else under the sun, but ultimately harms you, is that there is no such thing, and humanity, society, life, all of it requires the bad with the good, it can’t be fairy wine for. Every meal, and you know, the ever apple for every dinner, it has to be meaningful and human and flawed and complex in order to count and to be sustainable, and so that’s why this, you know, I, when I often think of fairies and fairy tables, I think there’s actually a scene from an early Holly Black book, I was a perfect age, by the way, to consume her literature as I was a teen. It was very impactful, but just this idea of like walking into a forest and by moonlight there is like a, you know, incredible glow, a party, a banquet, a table, it’s so beautiful. And then when you approach, you realize that you know there’s there’s something that they need from you that you might in the moment be tempted to give, but actually isn’t worth it, because the life you were dissatisfied with, or you know, wanted more for yourself from, is actually worth so much more than giving it all away for a fantasy.

JK

Interesting, or a quick, yeah, quick fix to their life, an unearned fix, I guess, which there would be consequences from. Okay, so I want to get into just a little fun exercise with you two, since you guys are probably the perfect people to ask. I think we can all give our answer here, but you’re a Fae in the Fae world, what food or drink based on you does someone consume to stay in your world?

JS

Oh, that’s, that’s a tough one. I, you know, what’s wild, and this is going to sound crazy, but you got to stay with me here. Bean dishes, I think, like a well-cooked bean dish is like phenomenal and out of this world, and usually seen as like a very humble food, but like imagine like the perfect cassoulet or like a perfect kind of like creamy bean like dip or like sauce or something like that, I’m like super into that. I really think that would be absolutely delicious.

AM

Julia, that is the most peasant-core Streganona stuff you have ever said, and I am into it.

JK

Honestly, that felt so mythologic just then. Like, that was that’s a beautiful answer. I’m like, yeah, of course.

AM

Okay, let me go totally different. Mine is gonna be like a light, buttery crostini with some mascarpone cheese, some like brandy poached pears, maybe a couple of like pomegranate seeds on top, maybe a little bit of basil or mint, some fresh herb, and I’m picturing this being like in the dead of winter, and yet we have like delicious summer peaches and fresh cheese and delicious herbs, buttery cracker. You just eat it forever.

JS

Okay, I will say, Amanda reminded me I was a cheese monger for 10 years. The perfect cheese board is actually the correct answer for me. I think a cheese board the size of a table is, is pretty much what it’s going to be.

AM

Yeah, like, if you’ve ever seen how beautiful they set the food on the TV show Hannibal, like, yes, but it’s just all cheese instead of, like, human flesh. You don’t know what the prosciutto is, Julia. Don’t worry about it.

JK

And that’s maybe one of the most irresistible things to humans currently, is the perfect cheese board, you guys just gave such beautiful answers, and my answer is so outside of that, but I think I can personally never resist the siren call of a nerd’s gummy cluster. So,

JS

Yes.

JK

So mine is definitely a nerd’s gummy cluster. You’re, you’re in the in-between world, and it’s actually an airport. Yeah,

JS

I think to elevate it a little, I think your land is just like the first room that they go into in Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory, you’re waking up the palette with all of that amazing citric acid.

JK

Colorful, sort of tastes like poison. Yeah, it’s bad for you. Unusual

AM

Unusual colors, I’m into it.

JK

Okay. Last one, you’re throwing a fairy party for your friends to celebrate May Day, Beltane. What wine are we, or what drinks are we serving? What are we having for dinner? What are we having for dessert?

JS

Oh man, so I’ll give two answers, because we’re a cocktail show, but I also obviously love wine. My cocktail answer is the last word, which is an equal parts cocktail. It is gin, lime juice, maraschino liqueur, and green chartreuse, which I think is the most Fae-coded liqueur that you can possibly get on the planet, only two people at a time know the recipe. It has like 160 different herbs. It’s a secret. It’s made by monks, like you don’t get more fake coated than that. In my humble opinion.

AM

Julia, it sounds a little otherworldly. I don’t know.

JS

That’s what I’m saying, that’s what I’m saying, and then for the wine, there is an incredible white wine that I’m kind of obsessed with right now. It is, I believe, called like Solgraphy, or Sol Graphy, and it is like oddly salinic, but it also tastes like fresh green apples, and has like a little bit of like a like sulfuric, like harvested from a volcano sort of taste to it, like it has a real terroir of like exactly where it was grown, and I absolutely love that wine, that is my go-to wine, especially now that the weather is warming up.

AM

Julia, how did you not mention that the maker is called Sons of Wine?

JS

Oh, because I totally forgot that.

AM

I think I would get an amazing, delicious orange wine, that’s been my go-to. I’m a trend girly, and orange wine has not left me since a few summers ago, and specifically, because we’re recording here on International Workers Day, I think I would try to source one from a wine cooperative from somewhere that people can do the wonderful risk sharing enterprise of going in together on costs and also profits. I think there’s nothing more human than that.

JK

I think I would go with, I can’t stop thinking about this wine. I tried it a couple of months ago. It’s an Austrian Sekt, which is sekt is the Austrian and German equivalent to a spark, their sparkling wine, if you will. And this particular one was, uh, made with Gruner Veltliner, which to me was so interesting, and it tasted like it tasted almost citrusy, a lot of green apples, so something more akin to like a sparkling, sparkling Sauvignon Blanc, and it was just the strangest, but best thing I’ve had in a long time, and I can’t stop thinking about, and I feel like that is very a Fae feeling,

JS

That’s how I felt the first time I had Lombresco, where I was like, it’s sparkling and also red. How is that possible?

JK

Little sweet? It’s so good.

AM

Yeah, that’s how I felt when we did a live show in Chicago, and someone served Malort to me on the stage, and I said, “we’re doing this”, and it felt like I fell down a rabbit hole

That’s from a nefarious fairy, for sure. 

JS

For sure, trickster energy.

AM

That’s why they only serve it in little two ounce glasses. You can’t take more than that.

Okay… What are we having for dinner?

Ah, okay. I think you have to do a sort of like wine braised meat of some kind, right? And I think it’s more mysterious if you don’t know what the meat is.

Julia said, “I’m bringing, I’m bringing Hannibal into it.

Okay. No, I am so sorry, because, like, talking about this, I realized that the book that I’m currently reading, which is called The Starving Saints by.. let me look that up real quick.. The Starving Saints by Caitlin Starling is so coded to this conversation that we’re having right now, and obviously I’m gonna say a little bit darker than ACOTAR is, but if you kind of like the darkness that happens in ACOTAR at times, a very interesting novel.

I’ll take that recommendation.

I kind of like the magic trick where a vegetable tastes like a steak, and so I think a great pairing for this amazing kind of like soft braised great textural mane would be some like seared grilled delicious herby seasonal steaks of like cauliflower turnip, you know, kohlrabi, like a bunch of stuff that we’d just really want to sink our teeth into.

I was thinking something, because I think gardening is magic, but if you’ve ever seen the squash flowers that people will serve, sometimes them with like ricotta and herbs and things like that, and they’re like this, like very, very savory, beautiful presentation too, that would probably get me.

And I think just to remind us of, you know, humanity, like whatever is seasonal and delicious in that moment, if we’re in like the week of the ramps, the week of the garlic flour, or whatever it might be, that’d be a great thing to include.

Great. And most importantly, what’s for dessert?

I am not a huge dessert person, however, I do like anything that is lemon flavored, so like a really like light lemon cheesecake, a sort of like super light, you know, I’ll, I say light, and then I say olive oil cake, but a light, like citrusy olive oil cake would also be totally my jam, with like maybe some crystallized rosemary or some other kind of herb on top.

I would totally fall for the fairy food if it was like an otherworldly yuzu key lime pie. I’m done. I’m done for.

I think something like… you mentioned olive oil, some like an olive oil ice cream with flaky sea salt over all over top, or maybe even like a lavender ice, yeah, something…

That one would get Julia.

Yeah, you got me, I’m in there. The Fae have me now.

Oh no, we’ve lost her.

What a way to go.

Worth it.

Well, I really want to thank both of you, for your time and for your knowledge. This, this was a lot of fun.

It was our pleasure. Thank you so much for having us.

Delightful, and y’all are welcome over our hearth and at our table anytime.

Okay, I’m joining. I want to thank Amanda and Julia for their time and magical answers.
